questions.stem: This four-panel image shows a tan-white membrane seen on endoscopy of the colon, a brown residue after its removal, and histology in which neutrophils appear to "erupt" from a crypt like a volcano. Infection by which organism most classically produces this picture?

explanation.summary: The tan-white pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris, together with the crypt "volcano" eruption of neutrophils, is the hallmark of pseudomembranous colitis caused by toxin-producing Clostridioides difficile, typically after antibiotic disruption of gut flora.
explanation.whyCorrect: The tan-white pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris, together with the crypt "volcano" eruption of neutrophils, is the hallmark of pseudomembranous colitis caused by toxin-producing Clostridioides difficile, typically after antibiotic disruption of gut flora.

questions.stem: These gross cardiac specimens show friable, bulky vegetations attached to the leaflets of a mitral valve and a bicuspid aortic valve. What is the underlying pathologic process?

explanation.summary: Friable, destructive valvular vegetations composed of organisms, fibrin, and inflammatory cells indicate infective endocarditis; Streptococcus viridans classically seeds abnormal or damaged valves while Staphylococcus aureus can attack even previously normal or congenitally bicuspid valves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Friable, destructive valvular vegetations composed of organisms, fibrin, and inflammatory cells indicate infective endocarditis; Streptococcus viridans classically seeds abnormal or damaged valves while Staphylococcus aureus can attack even previously normal or congenitally bicuspid valves.

questions.stem: This colon shows a thick tan-white membrane on endoscopy, with histology revealing 'volcano-like' eruptions of neutrophils and debris from the crypts. Which organism is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: A pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris erupting from colonic crypts ('volcano lesions') is characteristic of C. difficile colitis, typically after antibiotic disruption of normal flora; its toxins A and B damage the mucosa.
explanation.whyCorrect: A pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris erupting from colonic crypts ('volcano lesions') is characteristic of C. difficile colitis, typically after antibiotic disruption of normal flora; its toxins A and B damage the mucosa.

questions.stem: These heart valves show friable vegetations along the leaflet closure lines. What is the most likely underlying process producing these lesions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sterile fibrin deposition from a hypercoagulable state

explanation.summary: Friable vegetations on the valve leaflets are the hallmark of infective endocarditis, in which bacteria (e.g., S. viridans on the mitral valve, S. aureus on a congenitally bicuspid valve) seed and proliferate on the valve surface.
explanation.whyCorrect: Friable vegetations on the valve leaflets are the hallmark of infective endocarditis, in which bacteria (e.g., S. viridans on the mitral valve, S. aureus on a congenitally bicuspid valve) seed and proliferate on the valve surface.

questions.stem: This pharynx shows a thick gray membrane coating the tonsils and throat that bleeds when scraped away. The toxin of which organism produces this finding?

explanation.summary: Diphtheria toxin from C. diphtheriae produces a tightly adherent gray pseudomembrane of fibrin, dead cells, and inflammatory debris in the pharynx that can obstruct the airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diphtheria toxin from C. diphtheriae produces a tightly adherent gray pseudomembrane of fibrin, dead cells, and inflammatory debris in the pharynx that can obstruct the airway.
